text: Hello. I am sharing a low anterior for a rectal cancer after TNT.
This is a patient with a left pelvic kidney. That's what you were looking at there.
Due to the patient's high BMI, the surgeon uses an intracorporeal suturing technique as the Keith needle is unable to pass through the thick abdominal wall.
The surgeon mobilizes the sigmoid colon, which has lateral attachments on the right side, requiring careful manipulation to detach and reposition the colon.
I left this video largely unedited for the entire mobilization and TME dissection. I did not really have a set plan of what to expect or know what to do because I've never seen this before.
So I'm just taking my time and try not to make any mistakes.
The pelvic kidney is visible on the left, displacing the rectosigmoid mesentery to the right. The surgeon follows the right side of the TME plane.
The surgeon navigates the sigmoid mesentery, initially losing orientation, then corrects the approach by returning to the proper retro rectus space.
The surgeon navigates the retrorectal space over the promontory, identifying the superior hemorrhoidal vessel, which is visually displaced to the patient's right.
The surgeon works along the superior hemorrhoidal vessel, attempting to reach the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) while separating unusual right-sided lateral attachments to the sigmoid colon.
This obviously needs to be taken down in order to get length for the colloanal anastomosis. In terms of the cancer, this was a T three and two.
She underwent a total neoadjuvant therapy, which was chemotherapy followed by radiation
She had had a very good response to the treatment but still had persistent disease and presented for resection.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers underneath the superior hemorrhoidal vessel to prepare for its division, proceeding with the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection.
I do think that... ICG was helpful in this case to show me how far lateral the left ureter was. And so using it on and off really helped me to stay out of trouble.

The surgeon outlines the planned total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection, focusing on the anterior aspect within the rectovaginal septum, marking the area to aid in orientation and speed of the procedure.
The surgeon identifies the pelvic kidney and notes the ureter positioned laterally. They proceed to connect anatomical landmarks beneath the sigmoid mesentery, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies a visible window over the pelvic kidney, providing a clear view of the anatomical structure during the colectomy procedure.

The surgeon focuses on dissecting around the superior hemorrhoidal IMA pedicle, which is positioned more towards the patient's right side. The vessel sealer is used due to increased vascularity in the area. The ureter is visible extending laterally, and the surgeon aims to create a window around the pedicle within the mesentery.

The surgeon switches to scissors for precise dissection, targeting the superior hemorrhoidal vessel on the right. The left colic branch is identified and taken distal to the left colic to achieve necessary length, with the left colic also being dissected.
The surgeon identifies the inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) and proceeds with the total mesorectal excision (TME) dissection.

The surgeon navigates the left side of the surgical field, avoiding injury to the left-sided pelvic kidney. The dissection progresses circumferentially around the tissue, with the camera transitioning to arm number two and port hopping to the right for a direct view of the deeper pelvis.
The surgeon continues circumferential dissection in the wide pelvic area, navigating around anatomical structures with ease due to the spaciousness of the pelvis.

The surgeon performs anterior dissection, carefully avoiding the vagina while working circumferentially around the tumor located approximately six centimeters from the anal verge. A black tattoo is visible distal to the tumor at the level of the anorectal ring.

The surgeon performs a total mesorectal excision with colloanal anastomosis, using monopolar cautery to dissect the mesorectum from the pelvic floor. The camera is positioned in the epigastrium midline, providing a straight view into the deep pelvis, where the levators are visible on the patient's right side.
The surgeon uses monopolar scissors for the dissection in a wide pelvis, achieving good visualization and hemostasis without needing to switch to a bipolar energy device.

The surgeon continues dissection on the left side, mirroring the plane previously established on the right. The levators are visible on the patient's left, and the surgeon flips posteriorly for a better view, lifting the mesorectum off the pelvic levator floor. The tattoo near the rectal wall is visible by the left hand grasper. The surgeon prepares for stapling, aiming to get slightly lower, with the rectum and vagina being palpated before introducing the stapler.

The surgeon carefully avoids the vagina while completing the dissection. The proximal mesentery is taken, and the specimen is extracted through the upsized 12mm trocar in arm number three, minimizing the need for a separate extraction incision in the higher BMI patient.

The surgeon takes the mesentery intracorporeally to prevent tearing during extraction. The anvil is placed in a baker orientation, and the stapled rectum is visible withdrawn into the levator floor, indicating a low stapled anastomosis. The colon is brought in, and the surgeon staples while the robot holds the anvil. A leak test is performed, which is negative, followed by the creation of a diverting loop ileostomy. The final pathology shows a T2 N0 post-treatment tumor.
